answers.value:
Allopurinol – developed diffuse pruritic maculopapular rash and facial swelling within 10 days of initiation in 2020, medication permanently discontinued.
Colchicine – severe gastrointestinal intolerance (nausea, abdominal cramping, and diarrhea) even at low doses.
Lisinopril – persistent dry cough; switched to losartan with resolution of symptoms.
No known allergies to latex or contrast dye. No prior reactions to IV infusions other than mild transient flushing with IV steroids.

answers.value: Chronic tophaceous gout involving multiple joints with recurrent flares despite maximally tolerated oral urate-lowering therapy and documented allopurinol hypersensitivity.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Significant functional impairment of bilateral first MTP joints, ankles, and right elbow due to large tophi; baseline serum uric acid persistently > 9.0 mg/dL.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Patient has had 5 or more severe gout flares in the past 12 months requiring multiple courses of oral and intramuscular steroids, leading to missed work and progressive functional decline. Large, painful tophi over bilateral great toes, Achilles tendons, and right olecranon are now interfering with ambulation and ability to perform his job as a warehouse supervisor (difficulty climbing ladders, prolonged standing, and operating equipment). Serum uric acid has remained persistently elevated between 9.2–10.4 mg/dL over the last year despite maximally titrated febuxostat and intermittent colchicine (which he cannot tolerate due to GI side effects). Orthopedic surgery has expressed concern that, without more aggressive urate-lowering therapy, he may ultimately require joint-debulking procedures and possible joint replacement. Due to uncontrolled disease activity, high tophus burden, and the risk of further permanent joint damage and disability if treatment is delayed, we are requesting STAT initiation of KRYSTEXXA infusions to rapidly debulk urate burden and prevent additional structural joint damage.

answers.field_label: Labs To Be Drawn By Infusion Center Frequency
answers.value: CBC and CMP to be drawn at baseline (prior to first infusion) and then every 8 weeks while on KRYSTEXXA therapy, or more frequently if clinically indicated (e.g., new symptoms, suspected adverse reaction, or change in renal function). Please fax all lab results to Tulsa Rheumatology Associates at (918) 555-4811 within 24–48 hours of collection.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Premedication protocol per referring rheumatologist:
- 30–60 minutes prior to each KRYSTEXXA infusion, please administer:
  • Fexofenadine 180 mg PO (or equivalent non-sedating antihistamine if not available)
  • Acetaminophen 650 mg PO
  • Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV

Infusion instructions:
- Infuse KRYSTEXXA 8 mg IV over at least 120 minutes via peripheral IV line with appropriate in-line filtration per manufacturer guidelines.
- Monitor vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2, and temperature) prior to infusion, every 30 minutes during infusion, and for at least 60 minutes post-infusion.
- Have emergency medications and equipment readily available for potential infusion or anaphylactic reactions (e.g., epinephrine, IV antihistamines, IV steroids, oxygen, and airway equipment).
- If patient develops chest tightness, dyspnea, generalized flushing, urticaria, or significant blood pressure changes, stop the infusion immediately and manage per infusion reaction protocol; notify Dr. Whitaker’s office the same day.

Concomitant therapy:
- Continue febuxostat 80 mg PO daily until instructed otherwise by rheumatology.
- Patient uses prednisone 5–10 mg PO daily during flares; attempt to taper off as symptoms allow once uric acid begins to normalize.

Access and scheduling notes:
- Patient works early morning shifts (5:00 AM–1:30 PM) Monday–Friday and prefers infusion appointments after 2:00 PM on weekdays, ideally every other Wednesday.
- Mobility is limited during flares; allow extra time for ambulation and provide wheelchair assistance from the lobby if needed.

Communication preferences:
- Patient prefers phone calls for urgent matters and email for routine updates. Please also copy his wife, Laura Reynolds, on scheduling communications when possible (email: laura.reynolds.ok@gmail.com), as she frequently drives him to appointments.

Safety and prior history:
- No prior history of anaphylaxis to medications or infusions. Has tolerated IV methylprednisolone and IV antibiotics in the past without issue.
- Baseline renal function: eGFR ~68 mL/min/1.73 m², stable over last 12 months.

Please contact Jessica Moore, RN at Tulsa Rheumatology Associates at (918) 555-4810 for any questions regarding this order, infusion parameters, or if there are any concerns about lab results or adverse events.
